text: This is Dustin Baldwin. Today I'll be discussing a robotic recurrent right inguinal hernia repair. This is a patient who had a bilateral inguinal hernia repair done in the pre-peritoneal plane about a year ago by another provider. He presented to my clinic with complaints of a recurrent right inguinal hernia. I brought him to the OR today to examine and to repair it. As you can see on the left side, the repair looks good. On the right, he has this moderate-sized direct recurrence, which we're repairing. The operation begins the same way.

The surgeon operates in the preperitoneal plane, removing adhesions to access the direct hernia. A piece of pro-grip mesh is visible over the malpictinial orifice, surrounded by scar tissue. The surgeon works medially to reduce the direct hernia, revealing the top of the recurrent hernia while preserving the pseudosac. The pro-grip mesh is noted to be scarred under the inferior epigastric vessels, prompting the decision to avoid disturbing that area.

The surgeon works on reducing the direct inguinal hernia, peeling off the contents of the peritoneum and retroperitoneal fat. The attenuated transversalis fascia, or pseudosac, is visible. The dissection progresses down to Cooper's ligament, which is scarred from prior surgery. The mesh is visible, contributing to the adhesions being reduced.

The surgeon carefully dissects around Cooper's ligament, navigating through scar tissue from previous surgeries. The peritoneal sac and transversalis pseudosac are reduced, revealing the underlying anatomical structures. The challenging nature of the redo case requires modification of the closure technique.

The surgeon examines the femoral canal, identifying the femoral vein and carefully avoiding it. The mesh is heavily adhered to surrounding tissues, including the epigastric vessels, and the surgeon works laterally to separate the mesh from these structures.

The surgeon decides to stop the dissection and sew the direct defect closed using a two OV lock suture to reinforce the hernia repair. The dissected space measures approximately ten by ten centimeters. A square piece of non-absorbable mesh is cut and placed over the direct space, sutured to Cooper's ligament and the anterior abdominal wall for wide coverage. The mesh is anchored with a running V-lock suture, ensuring it covers the femoral space and extends laterally to the midline.

The surgeon performs a traditional closure of the flap using a 3-0 V-lock suture. The final view shows the peritoneum closed with the mesh laying flat in good position, without any buckling or wrinkling, indicating the hernia is completely reduced.
